result:
All confirmed directly from source (`fs/read_write.c`) plus cbm's call graph (which in this repo attaches `CALLS` edges to file nodes rather than function nodes — cbm's inbound trace on `vfs_read` correctly returned `fs/read_write.c` and `fs/exec.c` as the only callers, corroborating the chain below).

## Call chain: `read()` syscall → `vfs_read()`

1. **`SYSCALL_DEFINE3(read, unsigned int, fd, char __user *, buf, size_t, count)`** — `fs/read_write.c:621`
   Body: `return ksys_read(fd, buf, count);`
2. **`ksys_read(unsigned int fd, char __user *buf, size_t count)`** — `fs/read_write.c:602`
   Body: calls `fdget_pos()`/`file_ppos()`, then `ret = vfs_read(f.file, buf, count, ppos);`
3. **`vfs_read(struct file *file, char __user *buf, size_t count, loff_t *pos)`** — `fs/read_write.c:450`

Only 2 named-function hops between the syscall entry point and `vfs_read`: `SYSCALL_DEFINE3(read,...) → ksys_read → vfs_read`. (Note: `SYSCALL_DEFINE3` is a macro that expands to the actual entry symbol, e.g. `__x64_sys_read`/`__do_sys_read`, at line 621 — the graph tool doesn't resolve the macro expansion, but the body at that site is the code shown above.)

## Syscall-number → handler mapping (in-tree, non-generated)

`arch/x86/entry/syscalls/syscall_64.tbl:11`:
```
0	common	read			sys_read
```
This table is the hand-maintained (checked-in) source; the actual dispatch table (`sys_call_table`) is generated from it at build time via a script, so `syscall_64.tbl` is the authoritative in-tree source stating syscall number `0` on x86_64 maps to the `read` handler (`sys_read`, i.e. the `SYSCALL_DEFINE3(read,...)` symbol above).
